settings.title: Is my hair low porosity if it's frizzy?
settings.content: Yes — frizzy low porosity hair is very common, and it's particularly pronounced in the Philippines. The closed cuticles mean moisture doesn't get absorbed inside the shaft; instead it collects on the surface, where Manila's ambient humidity activates it as frizz. The fix is to seal the cuticle properly after washing with a lightweight oil — the Davines OI Milk, Davines OI Oil, or K18 Hair Oil work well here. Adding more moisturizing product is the opposite of what helps: it increases surface buildup and gives humidity more material to activate.

settings.title: What's the difference between low porosity and high porosity hair — and why does it matter for product choice in the Philippines?
settings.content: High porosity hair has raised, open cuticles that absorb moisture quickly but lose it just as fast — common after bleaching, rebonding, or heavy chemical treatments. Low porosity hair has sealed cuticles that resist both moisture absorption and moisture loss. In the Philippines, high porosity is typical in heavily processed hair (post-bleach, post-rebond), while low porosity is more common in naturally straight, dense Asian hair that has not been heavily chemically treated. Products are essentially reversed for each type: high-po hair benefits from heavy, coating treatments; low-po hair needs lightweight, humectant-first formulas applied with heat. Choosing the wrong type's products is the most common reason a routine stops working.

settings.title: Can I use K18 if I have low porosity hair?
settings.content: Yes — K18 is actually one of the best leave-in choices for low porosity hair precisely because of its molecular size. The K18Peptide™ is small enough to penetrate even tightly sealed cuticles that reject larger protein molecules. The non-negotiable is correct application: apply to towel-dried (not soaking wet) hair, wait the full 4 minutes, and do not rinse out. Do not follow with a heavy conditioner on top. Results build cumulatively — expect to see the full benefit after four or more consistent uses.

settings.title: What happens if I use heavy products on low porosity hair?
settings.content: Heavy oils like coconut and castor, thick protein masks, and silicone-rich conditioners sit on the surface of low porosity cuticles and accumulate quickly. The result: hair that feels heavy and looks greasy but is paradoxically dry inside, because nothing actually penetrated. Hair becomes difficult to style, loses volume, and feels perpetually coated. If your hair currently feels this way, step one is a clarifying shampoo reset. After that, switch to lightweight, humectant-rich formulas layered with the LCO method — liquid first, then a light cream, then a lightweight oil to seal — and give your hair two to three wash cycles to recalibrate.

settings.title: Should I do a protein treatment on low porosity hair?
settings.content: Be careful. Standard protein treatments use large keratin molecules that cannot pass through sealed low porosity cuticles — they add coating rather than structural repair. If your hair is also chemically treated (rebonded, colored), you may need some protein, but choose hydrolyzed protein, which has a smaller molecular weight, or a targeted repair product like the K18 Leave-In Mask over a heavy rinsed-out protein mask. K18 is a better choice than most traditional protein treatments for low-po hair specifically because of its molecular design. If you do use a protein treatment, limit contact time — the cuticle won't open to receive a mask that's left on for 30+ minutes and the excess will just sit on the surface.
